title: Stone-safe cleaning spray
subtitle: A pH-neutral castile soap spray for natural stone surfaces
excerpt: Marble, limestone, and travertine are dissolved by acids, including vinegar and citric acid. This spray uses only pH-neutral castile soap diluted in water with tea tree essential oil. It cleans and deodorises without etching or dulling the stone surface.
sourceNotes: Natural stone cleaning from the Marble Institute of America care guide (marble-institute.com, May 2026). Castile soap dilution rates from DIY Natural (diynatural.com, May 2026). Tea tree EO from Tisserand Institute (tisserandinstitute.org, May 2026).

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Do not increase the castile soap ratio: too much soap leaves a film on stone that dulls the surface.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up a full spray bottle at a time. Label clearly as 'stone safe'. Do not mix with acid-based cleaners.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 3 months in a labelled spray bottle. The castile soap base is the limiting factor. Discard if the spray develops a rancid smell or visibly separates.

glossaryTerms.definition: The chemical reaction between an acid and calcium carbonate in natural stone, which dissolves the surface and leaves dull, rough patches. Vinegar, lemon juice, citric acid, and many commercial bathroom cleaners contain acids that etch marble, limestone, and travertine. Once etched, the stone requires professional re-polishing. Use only pH-neutral products on natural stone.

content.text: Natural stone surfaces: marble, limestone, travertine, and some granites: are permanently damaged by acidic cleaners. Even short exposure to vinegar or citric acid causes
that shows as dull patches on a polished surface. This spray uses only diluted castile soap, which is alkaline rather than acidic, and is safe for regular use on all natural stone.

Keep this spray clearly labelled and stored separately from any acid-based cleaners. Never mix the two: castile soap and acid form a curdled, oily mess that can clog drain pipes. Tea tree essential oil is toxic to cats. For a pet-safe version, substitute 10 drops of lavender essential oil in place of the tea tree.

items.prepNote: Distilled or filtered water; tap water mineral content can leave marks on polished stone
Concentrated; the surfactant; use only a small amount to avoid soapy residue
Approximately 1 ml; antimicrobial; optional: omit for a fully scent-free version

content.text: Add the castile liquid soap to the spray bottle first, then add the distilled water: adding water first causes excessive foaming. Add the tea tree essential oil. Cap and shake gently. Label the bottle clearly.

To use: spray onto the stone surface, wipe with a soft damp cloth, and dry immediately with a clean dry cloth. Do not leave standing water on polished stone. For heavy residue, allow the spray to sit for 2 minutes before wiping.
3 months. Keep separate from acid cleaners. Shake gently before each use. Discard if the spray turns cloudy or smells off.
